impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's conditioning of patent licenses on customers' exclusive purchase of unpatented products constitutes unlawful tying under the Sherman Act when the unpatented products have substantial non-infringing uses.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding Ingevity liable for unlawful tying under the Sherman Act. Ingevity conditioned licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers agreeing to exclusively purchase Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld the finding that the honeycombs had substantial non-infringing uses (e.g., in air-intake systems outside the patent's scope), defeating Ingevity's defense that they were protected 'nonstaple goods' under patent law.
impacts.positiveImplications: Clarifies that competitors can challenge patent holders who leverage patent licenses to force exclusive purchases of unpatented goods, providing a pathway to antitrust relief against anticompetitive bundling practices.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face significant antitrust liability risk when conditioning patent licenses on purchases of unpatented products, even when the products are related to the patented technology, if those products have substantial non-infringing uses.

impacts.caseName: Trustees of Columbia University v. Gen Digital Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to detecting anomalous program executions by comparing function calls made in an emulator to a model of expected function calls are abstract under step one of the Alice framework, and whether foreign sales damages were properly awarded.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated a $185 million jury verdict for Columbia, holding that the asserted claims of cybersecurity patents were abstract at Alice step one and remanding for step two analysis. The court also held that the district court erred in allowing damages based on Norton's foreign sales. The decision significantly impacts software and cybersecurity patent enforcement and limits recovery for foreign sales in patent cases.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies benefit from strengthened patent eligibility defenses against broadly-claimed software patents, and the foreign sales damages ruling limits patentees' ability to recover damages for overseas revenues.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders, particularly universities and research institutions licensing cybersecurity and software patents, face heightened risk that their claims will be deemed abstract under Alice, potentially invalidating significant patent portfolios.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to a server that tailors content presentation specifications to a wireless device's screen size and capabilities are ineligible for patenting under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that GoTV's patents claiming methods and systems for server-side tailoring of content to wireless device capabilities were ineligible under § 101, reversing a jury verdict of infringement and $2.5 million damages award against Netflix. The court found the claims directed to the abstract idea of adapting content for device display. This ruling required entry of judgment for Netflix across all asserted patents.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology and streaming companies gain strong precedent against patent assertions based on broadly-claimed adaptive content delivery methods, reducing exposure to patent licensing demands for routine content optimization.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with claims directed to content adaptation and device-specific rendering face significant invalidation risk under § 101, even where claims include specific technical implementations.

impacts.caseName: REGENXBIO Inc. v. Sarepta Therapeutics, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether claims directed to genetically engineered host cells containing recombinant nucleic acid molecules encoding a specific AAV capsid protein and a heterologous non-AAV sequence are patent-ineligible as directed to a natural phenomenon under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's grant of summary judgment invalidating gene therapy patent claims under § 101, holding that cultured host cells containing engineered recombinant nucleic acid molecules are not directed to a natural phenomenon. The court found the claims cover man-made compositions that do not exist in nature. This is a significant ruling for the biotechnology and gene therapy industries, preserving patent protection for engineered biological constructs.
impacts.positiveImplications: Biotechnology and gene therapy companies gain important validation that engineered host cells and recombinant constructs are patent-eligible, protecting substantial R&D investments in gene therapy platforms.
impacts.negativeImplications: Competitors in the gene therapy space face continued exposure to patent infringement claims covering engineered biological materials, potentially increasing licensing costs.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether genuine disputes of material fact precluded summary judgment on inequitable conduct in obtaining patents and on a Walker Process fraud claim alleging attempted monopolization through enforcement of a fraudulently obtained patent.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgment on both sides, finding genuine disputes of material fact regarding whether Tenaris committed inequitable conduct in obtaining coiled tubing patents by concealing prior art (the CYMAX Documents), and whether Global Tubing could sustain a Walker Process antitrust fraud claim for attempted monopolization. The case involves the intersection of patent procurement fraud and antitrust liability.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies accused of patent infringement can pursue Walker Process antitrust counterclaims when there is evidence the patent holder concealed material prior art, providing a powerful defensive and offensive tool.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face increased litigation risk when prior art known during prosecution is not disclosed, as both inequitable conduct and antitrust monopolization claims may survive summary judgment.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a $125 million class action settlement of claims that the federal judiciary overcharged PACER users was properly approved, including the settlement terms, attorneys' fees, and incentive awards to class representatives.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed approval of a $125 million nationwide class action settlement resolving claims that the federal judiciary exceeded its statutory authority by using PACER fees to fund expenses unrelated to PACER operations. The court upheld the settlement terms providing full or substantial reimbursement to hundreds of thousands of PACER users, as well as attorneys' fees and incentive awards to the three nonprofit class representatives.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses and law firms that are heavy PACER users will receive reimbursement of overcharged fees. The ruling also confirms the viability of class settlements involving government fee overcharges under the Little Tucker Act.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding a patent plaintiff's damages expert report and granting summary judgment for absence of a remedy based on that exclusion.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of Exafer's damages expert report in a patent infringement case against Microsoft's Azure platform and vacated the resulting summary judgment. The court found the district court applied an overly stringent standard in excluding the damages testimony, improperly depriving Exafer of its ability to prove a remedy for alleged infringement of network optimization patents.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent plaintiffs benefit from a more favorable standard for admitting damages expert testimony, reducing the risk that technical patent cases will be dismissed solely on Daubert grounds before trial.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies face increased exposure to patent damages claims as the bar for excluding damages experts is clarified as less stringent, potentially increasing settlement pressure.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC correctly found that Apple violated Section 337 of the Tariff Act by importing Apple Watch models incorporating blood oxygen measurement functionality covered by Masimo's patents.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the ITC's finding that Apple violated Section 337 by importing Apple Watches with blood oxygen features that infringed Masimo's patents, upholding the limited exclusion order barring importation. The court found no error in the Commission's domestic industry determination, validity rulings, or infringement findings. This high-profile decision directly impacts Apple's ability to sell certain Watch models in the United States.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders with legitimate ITC complaints gain confidence that exclusion orders will be upheld even against the largest technology companies, reinforcing the ITC as a powerful venue for patent enforcement.
impacts.negativeImplications: Major technology companies importing products with patented features face significant commercial disruption from ITC exclusion orders, potentially requiring costly design-arounds or licensing agreements.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the Patent Trial and Appeal Board governing discretionary denial of inter partes review institution constitute a 'general statement of policy' exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking requirements.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions guiding PTAB discretionary denials of IPR institution are general statements of policy exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking under APA § 553(b). The court held that because Congress provided no legal right to IPR institution and the Director retains ultimate authority over institution decisions, the instructions need not go through formal rulemaking. This preserves the Director's broad discretion to manage IPR proceedings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the PTO's flexibility to manage IPR institution, as discretionary denial policies remain insulated from judicial and procedural challenges.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies relying on IPRs to challenge patents face continued uncertainty about institution, as the Director's discretionary denial policies cannot be challenged as procedurally defective for lack of notice-and-comment rulemaking.

impacts.caseName: US Patent No. 7,679,637 LLC v. Google LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to web conferencing systems with time-shifting capabilities enabling asynchronous viewing of data streams are ineligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the district court's dismissal of a patent infringement suit against Google for failure to state a claim, finding that claims directed to web conferencing systems with time-shifting and asynchronous viewing capabilities were patent-ineligible under § 101. This decision further narrows the scope of enforceable software patents in the collaboration and streaming technology space.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies operating web conferencing and streaming platforms benefit from reduced patent exposure for implementing standard time-shifting and asynchronous playback features.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with claims covering web conferencing and multimedia streaming face heightened invalidity risk under § 101, limiting monetization of such patent portfolios.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations as confidential is consistent with the governing statute and whether § 1516a abrogates the common law right of access to judicial records.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the Court of International Trade's ruling that the ITC's blanket practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential in trade investigations is incompatible with the statute. The court held that 19 U.S.C. § 1516a does not abrogate the common law right of public access to judicial records. This requires the ITC to make individualized confidentiality determinations rather than applying automatic blanket designations.
impacts.positiveImplications: Importers and domestic competitors gain greater transparency in trade remedy proceedings, enabling better-informed legal strategies and public accountability in antidumping and countervailing duty cases.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies that submit proprietary business information in ITC questionnaires face increased risk of disclosure, potentially requiring more careful treatment of confidential business data in trade proceedings.

impacts.caseName: 112 Genesee Street, LLC v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether restaurants that applied for but did not receive Restaurant Revitalization Fund grants have Tucker Act jurisdiction to sue the United States for damages when the SBA failed to award grants in the statutorily mandated order.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that over 300 restaurants and businesses have Tucker Act jurisdiction to pursue claims against the United States for unpaid Restaurant Revitalization Fund grants. The court held that Congress's use of mandatory language ('shall award grants') created a money-mandating obligation requiring the SBA to award grants in the order applications were received, establishing a viable claim for damages when the SBA processed applications out of order.
impacts.positiveImplications: Small businesses denied government grants due to administrative processing errors have a viable path to recover damages, establishing that mandatory grant distribution language creates enforceable rights.
impacts.negativeImplications: The federal government faces potential liability exposure for failing to administer grant programs in strict compliance with statutory distribution orders, which may affect how agencies manage high-demand funding programs.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding expert testimony on induced patent infringement and erred in granting judgment as a matter of law to the defendant on induced infringement claims.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of two expert witnesses and JMOL for DePuy in a case involving spinal surgery device patents. The court found the district court improperly excluded expert testimony on how DePuy induced surgeons to infringe, requiring a new trial. This decision is significant for medical device patent enforcement, particularly where induced infringement depends on expert testimony about surgical techniques.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in the medical device space benefit from a more permissive standard for expert testimony in induced infringement cases, facilitating enforcement against device manufacturers who instruct surgeons in infringing methods.
impacts.negativeImplications: Medical device companies face greater litigation risk as expert testimony on induced infringement will be harder to exclude, increasing the likelihood of trials and potential damages awards.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether means-plus-function patent claims describing a 'control means' for a crop harvester header height system were properly held invalid as indefinite when the specification disclosed conventional controller components.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's finding that asserted claims of a crop harvester patent were invalid as indefinite and remanded for further proceedings. The court held that the specification's disclosure of conventional controller components provided sufficient corresponding structure for the means-plus-function 'control means' limitation, rejecting Deere's invalidity defense. This reinstates a patent infringement case involving agricultural technology.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders with means-plus-function claims benefit from the court's recognition that disclosing conventional components in the specification can satisfy the definiteness requirement, preserving enforceability of such claims.
impacts.negativeImplications: Agricultural equipment and other technology companies face renewed infringement exposure where means-plus-function claims reference conventional structures described in patent specifications.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC properly entered a limited exclusion order against defaulting respondents under 19 U.S.C. § 1337(g)(1) based on presumed facts while finding no violation by active respondents, and whether Crocs's appeal of the no-violation finding was timely.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit dismissed Crocs's appeal of the ITC's no-violation finding against active respondents as untimely, but affirmed the Commission's limited exclusion order against defaulting respondents who imported casual footwear allegedly infringing Crocs's 3D trade dress marks. The court upheld the ITC's application of the statutory presumption of infringement for defaulting parties under § 1337(g)(1), while the no-violation finding for active respondents (based on failure to prove likelihood of confusion) stands.
impacts.positiveImplications: Brand owners benefit from the confirmation that ITC default provisions provide automatic exclusion relief against non-participating importers, strengthening enforcement against counterfeit or infringing imports.
impacts.negativeImplications: Brand owners must strictly comply with ITC appeal deadlines, as untimely appeals will be dismissed regardless of the merits, and trade dress claims remain difficult to prove against active respondents on likelihood of confusion grounds.
